paper_title: Pollen analysis of honey and pollen collected by Apis mellifera linnaeus, 1758 (Hymenoptera, Apidae), in a mixed environment of Eucalyptus plantation and native cerrado in Southeastern Brazil.

methods.method_name: Plant Sample Collection and Preparation for Reference
methods.description: Process for collecting flowering plant samples from the study area, identifying them, preparing pollen slides for a reference collection, and depositing samples and slides.

steps.description: Samples of flowering-plant species were collected monthly, between January 2004 and January 2005, along firebreak margins and inside Cerrado reserves.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect flowering plant samples monthly from specified locations over a defined period.

steps.description: The plants were identified by the staff of the herbarium of the Taxonomic Collections of the Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais (BHCB)
steps.searchable_summary: Identify collected plant samples to species level.

steps.description: and deposited in the Laboratório de Palinologia of the Fundação Ezequiel Dias (FUNED), in Belo Horizonte, Minas Gerais, Brazil.
steps.searchable_summary: Deposit identified plant samples in a specified herbarium.

steps.description: Pollen collected from the anthers of these plants were acetolized according to Erdtman (1960)
steps.searchable_summary: Extract pollen from plant anthers and process using acetolysis method.
Prepare microscope slides from acetolized pollen.

steps.description: which were deposited in the FUNED-POL reference slide collection, where pollen of plants previously collected in the Fazenda Brejão were already represented.
steps.searchable_summary: Deposit prepared pollen slides in a reference collection.
outputs: Deposited pollen slides (archived in reference collection)

methods.description: Microscopic identification and counting of pollen grains from honey and pollen samples collected by bees, followed by classification into frequency classes according to melissopalynological standards.

steps.description: The identification of the pollen grains, in the previously prepared slides, was obtained through morphological analysis under the microscope
steps.searchable_summary: Identify pollen types based on morphology under a microscope.

steps.description: comparing with pollen descriptions from Erdtman (1952) and Laboriau (1973) and through comparison with the reference slides in the FUNED-POL reference pollen collection.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare observed pollen morphology with reference descriptions and slides for confirmation.

steps.description: The frequency of each pollen type in 1200 pollen grains counted in each slide was calculated.
steps.searchable_summary: Count a fixed number of pollen grains per slide and calculate the frequency of each type.
outputs: Raw counts of each pollen type per slide
Relative frequency (%) of each pollen type per slide

steps.description: Pollen grains identified in the honey samples were grouped in four frequency classes according to the usual proceedings in melissopalynology, as suggested by Louveaux et al. (1970) and Barth (1970a, b, c): predominant pollen (representing more than 45% of all grains in the sample); accessory pollen (representing between 15% and 44% of the grains); isolated pollen (between 3% and 14%) Pollen collected by Apis mellifera and occasional isolated pollen (less than 3%).
steps.searchable_summary: Assign pollen types from honey samples to frequency classes based on percentage thresholds.
inputs: Relative frequency (%) of each pollen type (from honey samples)
Frequency class definitions (Predominant >45%, Accessory 15-44%, Isolated 3-14%, Occasional Isolated <3%)
Melissopalynology standards (Louveaux et al. 1970, Barth 1970a, b, c)
outputs: Pollen types assigned to frequency classes

steps.description: Sub and over-representation of specific pollen types were considered in the data interpretation (Barth, 1989).
steps.searchable_summary: Account for potential sub/over-representation of pollen types during data interpretation.
